commit 91f34c42aef6e45e534fa70e0a72f2e6b5f26358
Author: Hao Ge <hao.ge@linux.dev>

mm/alloc_tag: clear codetag for pages allocated before page_ext initialization

This commit addresses an issue where pages allocated before page_ext is
fully initialized lack codetags, which triggers warnings when they are
later freed. It introduces a global array to track these early allocations
and clears their tags once page_ext initialization is complete.

> diff --git a/lib/alloc_tag.c b/lib/alloc_tag.c
> --- a/lib/alloc_tag.c
> +++ b/lib/alloc_tag.c
[ ... ]
> +void alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn)
> +{
> +	unsigned long flags;
> +
> +	if (mem_profiling_state != DOWN)
> +		return;
> +
> +	spin_lock_irqsave(&early_pfn_lock, flags);

Since this is called from the generic __pgalloc_tag_add() post-allocation
hook, can it be invoked from NMI context? If an NMI triggers an allocation
and interrupts a CPU already holding early_pfn_lock, will attempting to
acquire the same lock result in a deadlock?

> +	if (early_pfn_count >= EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX) {
> +		spin_unlock_irqrestore(&early_pfn_lock, flags);
> +		return;
> +	}

EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX is hardcoded to 256. If subsystems like SLUB fallback
trigger more than 256 allocations before page_ext is initialized, the array
will overflow and silently drop the remaining PFNs. Will these dropped
pages fail to have their tags cleared, eventually triggering the exact
warning this patch intends to fix?

[ ... ]
> +static void __init clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void)
> +{
> +	unsigned int i;
> +
> +	for (i = 0; i < early_pfn_count; i++) {
> +		unsigned long pfn = early_pfns[i];
> +
> +		if (pfn_valid(pfn)) {
> +			struct page *page = pfn_to_page(pfn);
> +
> +			clear_page_tag_ref(page);

If an early page is freed and then reallocated after its section's page_ext
is initialized but before this loop runs, the reallocation will set a valid
tag. Does clear_page_tag_ref() unconditionally overwrite this valid tag
with CODETAG_EMPTY, causing an accounting leak when the page is later freed?

Additionally, when CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG is enabled,
CODETAG_EMPTY is defined as ((void *)1). If one of these pages is later
split via pgalloc_tag_split() or migrated via pgalloc_tag_swap(),
__pgalloc_tag_get() checks if (ref.ct).

Since ((void *)1) evaluates to true, it will return a bogus pointer. Could
dereferencing this pointer to update counters cause a kernel panic?

[ ... ]
> @@ -729,6 +806,7 @@ static int __init setup_early_mem_profiling(char *str)
>  			compressed = true;
>  		}
>  		mem_profiling_support = true;
> +		mem_profiling_state = UP;

If profiling is explicitly enabled via the boot parameter
(sysctl.vm.mem_profiling), mem_profiling_state is set to UP here during
early boot argument parsing. Does this mean mem_profiling_is_available()
will return true before page_ext is actually allocated and initialized?

If so, early allocations will skip alloc_tag_add_early_pfn() and their tags
will never be recorded or cleared.

[ ... ]
> @@ -760,6 +838,8 @@ static __init bool need_page_alloc_tagging(void)
>  
>  static __init void init_page_alloc_tagging(void)
>  {
> +	mem_profiling_state = UP;
> +	clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs();
>  }

Is it possible for a concurrent allocation on another CPU to read
mem_profiling_state as DOWN but get delayed before taking early_pfn_lock?

Since clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs() resets early_pfn_count without
holding the lock, could the delayed allocation append its PFN after the
array is cleared, resulting in its tag never being reset?

commit 91f34c42aef6e45e534fa70e0a72f2e6b5f26358
Author: Hao Ge <hao.ge@linux.dev>
Date:   Thu Mar 19 08:31:53 2026 +0000

    mm/alloc_tag: clear codetag for pages allocated before page_ext initialization
    
    Due to initialization ordering, page_ext is allocated and initialized
    relatively late during boot. Some pages have already been allocated
    and freed before page_ext becomes available, leaving their codetag
    uninitialized.
    
    A clear example is in init_section_page_ext(): alloc_page_ext() calls
    kmemleak_alloc(). If the slab cache has no free objects, it falls back
    to the buddy allocator to allocate memory. However, at this point page_ext
    is not yet fully initialized, so these newly allocated pages have no
    codetag set. These pages may later be reclaimed by KASAN,which causes
    the warning to trigger when they are freed because their codetag ref is
    still empty.
    
    Use a global array to track pages allocated before page_ext is fully
    initialized, similar to how kmemleak tracks early allocations.
    When page_ext initialization completes, set their codetag
    to empty to avoid warnings when they are freed later.

diff --git a/include/linux/alloc_tag.h b/include/linux/alloc_tag.h
index d40ac39bfbe8..941bb4e40d3f 100644
--- a/include/linux/alloc_tag.h
+++ b/include/linux/alloc_tag.h
@@ -74,6 +74,9 @@ static inline void set_codetag_empty(union codetag_ref *ref)
 
 #ifdef CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING
 
+bool mem_profiling_is_available(void);
+void alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn);
+
 #define ALLOC_TAG_SECTION_NAME	"alloc_tags"
 
 struct codetag_bytes {
diff --git a/lib/alloc_tag.c b/lib/alloc_tag.c
index 58991ab09d84..a5bf4e72c154 100644
--- a/lib/alloc_tag.c
+++ b/lib/alloc_tag.c
@@ -6,6 +6,7 @@
 #include <linux/kallsyms.h>
 #include <linux/module.h>
 #include <linux/page_ext.h>
+#include <linux/pgalloc_tag.h>
 #include <linux/proc_fs.h>
 #include <linux/seq_buf.h>
 #include <linux/seq_file.h>
@@ -26,6 +27,82 @@ static bool mem_profiling_support;
 
 static struct codetag_type *alloc_tag_cttype;
 
+/*
+ * State of the alloc_tag
+ *
+ * This is used to describe the states of the alloc_tag during bootup.
+ *
+ * When we need to allocate page_ext to store codetag, we face an
+ * initialization timing problem:
+ *
+ * Due to initialization order, pages may be allocated via buddy system
+ * before page_ext is fully allocated and initialized. Although these
+ * pages call the allocation hooks, the codetag will not be set because
+ * page_ext is not yet available.
+ *
+ * When these pages are later free to the buddy system, it triggers
+ * warnings because their codetag is actually empty if
+ * CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG is enabled.
+ *
+ * Additionally, in this situation, we cannot record detailed allocation
+ * information for these pages.
+ */
+enum mem_profiling_state {
+	DOWN,			/* No mem_profiling functionality yet */
+	UP			/* Everything is working */
+};
+
+static enum mem_profiling_state mem_profiling_state = DOWN;
+
+bool mem_profiling_is_available(void)
+{
+	return mem_profiling_state == UP;
+}
+
+#ifdef CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG
+
+#define EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX		256
+
+static unsigned long early_pfns[EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX];
+static unsigned int early_pfn_count;
+static DEFINE_SPINLOCK(early_pfn_lock);
+
+void alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn)
+{
+	unsigned long flags;
+
+	if (mem_profiling_state != DOWN)
+		return;
+
+	spin_lock_irqsave(&early_pfn_lock, flags);
+	if (early_pfn_count >= EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX) {
+		spin_unlock_irqrestore(&early_pfn_lock, flags);
+		return;
+	}
+	early_pfns[early_pfn_count++] = pfn;
+	spin_unlock_irqrestore(&early_pfn_lock, flags);
+}
+
+static void __init clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void)
+{
+	unsigned int i;
+
+	for (i = 0; i < early_pfn_count; i++) {
+		unsigned long pfn = early_pfns[i];
+
+		if (pfn_valid(pfn)) {
+			struct page *page = pfn_to_page(pfn);
+
+			clear_page_tag_ref(page);
+		}
+	}
+	early_pfn_count = 0;
+}
+#else /* !CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG */
+inline void alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn) {}
+static inline void clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void) {}
+#endif
+
 #ifdef CONFIG_ARCH_MODULE_NEEDS_WEAK_PER_CPU
 DEFINE_PER_CPU(struct alloc_tag_counters, _shared_alloc_tag);
 EXPORT_SYMBOL(_shared_alloc_tag);
@@ -729,6 +806,7 @@ static int __init setup_early_mem_profiling(char *str)
 			compressed = true;
 		}
 		mem_profiling_support = true;
+		mem_profiling_state = UP;
 		pr_info("Memory allocation profiling is enabled %s compression and is turned %s!\n",
 			compressed ? "with" : "without", str_on_off(enable));
 	}
@@ -760,6 +838,8 @@ static __init bool need_page_alloc_tagging(void)
 
 static __init void init_page_alloc_tagging(void)
 {
+	mem_profiling_state = UP;
+	clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs();
 }
 
 struct page_ext_operations page_alloc_tagging_ops = {
diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
index ee81f5c67c18..3354032a38a4 100644
--- a/mm/page_alloc.c
+++ b/mm/page_alloc.c
@@ -1255,6 +1255,13 @@ void __pgalloc_tag_add(struct page *page, struct task_struct *task,
 		alloc_tag_add(&ref, task->alloc_tag, PAGE_SIZE * nr);
 		update_page_tag_ref(handle, &ref);
 		put_page_tag_ref(handle);
+	} else {
+		/*
+		 * page_ext is not available yet, record the pfn so we can
+		 * clear the tag ref later when page_ext is initialized.
+		 */
+		if (!mem_profiling_is_available())
+			alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(page_to_pfn(page));
 	}
 }

    "thought": "**Summary of the Memory Profiling Patch Analysis**

Alright, let's break down this commit. The core of it seems to be addressing a problem where we're seeing spurious warnings related to memory allocation profiling because pages are allocated before the `page_ext` structure is fully initialized. The root cause is `alloc_page_ext()`, which sometimes falls back to the buddy allocator if the slab cache is empty. This means the pages allocated by the buddy allocator before `page_ext` is available don't have their `codetag` initialized, and when they are later freed, a warning occurs.

My understanding is this patch attempts to solve this with a mechanism to track pages allocated early, before `page_ext` initialization completes. Specifically, it uses a global array, `early_pfns`, to store the physical frame numbers (PFNs) of these early allocations. The logic: when `page_ext` *is* initialized, it iterates through `early_pfns` and clears the tag references of those pages. The idea is to pre-empt the warnings.

However, after digging into the code, I've got some serious concerns. The approach introduces a race condition and a hardcoded limit that, I'm afraid, makes the fix less robust and possibly incorrect.

The core problem, as I see it, centers around the interaction between the early allocation tracking and the potential for a page to be reallocated *after* `page_ext` becomes available but *before* the `early_pfns` array is processed. Let me spell it out:

1.  A page is allocated early (before `page_ext` is ready), and its PFN is added to `early_pfns`.
2.  The page is then freed, and is likely still in the buddy allocator.
3.  Later, after `page_ext` is ready for all sections in memory, the page is *reallocated* for some other purpose. At this point, `page_ext` is available, so the new allocation sets its `codetag` and allocates valid memory.
4.  Then, *after* this reallocation, the code iterates through `early_pfns` and *unconditionally* clears the `codetag` on the page that was re-allocated. It blindly sets it to `CODETAG_EMPTY`.
5.  When this now-modified page is *freed* again, the `CODETAG_EMPTY` triggers a memory leak report, because the counters are not decremented.

This means the fix would *introduce* a memory profiling issue. The patch corrupts the allocation counters because a page's tag ref, which had profiling information, is cleared, and its deallocation counter won't be decremented later.

Adding to this issue is the implementation of the `early_pfns` array itself. It has a hardcoded size of 256. If more than 256 pages are allocated early (which is completely reasonable, given that the buddy allocator might be called a significant number of times while initializing), the code just silently *drops* the excess PFNs. This defeats the purpose of tracking those pages, and when the dropped pages are freed, they *still* produce the very warnings this patch is supposed to fix. This is a very real problem. And the larger the system, or the lower the memory pressure, the more likely the issue. This is a very serious design flaw.

I was also concerned about concurrent access to `early_pfns` during initialization. However, it seems that there is single-thread at this point in the boot. However, there is no guarantee that there will not be any interrupts or another CPU. So, there is still a slight race condition possible.

Basically, the patch introduces a data race that can corrupt the memory allocation accounting. The hardcoded limit of `early_pfns` makes the fix incomplete and unreliable, or, worse, actually harmful. In effect, it would make memory profiling data unreliable. The issue is that the code is modifying tags blindly, and it should check if the tag is valid before clearing it, to prevent race conditions. The fix should check if the tag is already set before clearing it.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Page Allocation Tagging Patch**

Okay, so the commit's goal is to track page allocations happening *before* `page_ext` is fully initialized, much like what kmemleak does. The plan is to store the page frame numbers (PFNs) of these early allocations in a global array – limited to 256 entries – and then, after `page_ext` is up and running, clear the associated tags to avoid spurious warnings later when those pages are freed.

Looking at the patch itself, I see a couple of new functions and a state variable being introduced. `alloc_tag.h` introduces `mem_profiling_is_available()` and `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()`, which is good. The `alloc_tag.c` file defines the `mem_profiling_state` enum, the `mem_profiling_is_available()` function (that should be pretty straightforward), and `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()`. This last function is the critical one; it stores the PFNs in the `early_pfns` array, protected by `early_pfn_lock`. The array size is constrained by `EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX`, which is 256, just as the commit message stated.

I see `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` too, which iterates through the `early_pfns` array. For each PFN, it validates it using `pfn_valid()`, then converts the PFN to a `struct page` pointer with `pfn_to_page()`. Finally, it calls `clear_page_tag_ref(page)`. This function is then called from within `init_page_alloc_tagging()`, which is what sets the `mem_profiling_state` to `UP`.

Now, the interesting bit is in `page_alloc.c`, specifically the `__pgalloc_tag_add()` function. Here's how it's supposed to work: If `get_page_tag_ref()` succeeds, it means `page_ext` is available. It does the usual tagging. But, if `get_page_tag_ref()` *fails* (meaning `page_ext` isn't ready), and `mem_profiling_is_available()` returns false, then it calls `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()` with the page's PFN.

A potential issue comes to mind when I'm thinking about the way this handles allocations of multiple pages, especially for larger orders (greater than 0). When `__pgalloc_tag_add` is called for a page allocation with order > 0, does `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` just add the base PFN? *Yes, `page_to_pfn(page)` is the base PFN.* Okay, that's what I was assuming. That's fine, but...what about `clear_page_tag_ref(page)`? Does that function clear the tag for *all* pages in the allocation, or just the base page? I need to check. If `nr` is the number of pages (e.g. `1 << order`), `clear_page_tag_ref(page)` might *only* clear the base page's tag? I need to verify what `clear_page_tag_ref` actually *does*.

Let me check the code.

```c
static inline void clear_page_tag_ref(struct page *page)
{
	if (mem_alloc_profiling_enabled())
		__clear_page_tag_ref(page);
}
```

Subsequent checks confirm that `is_codetag_empty(ref)` sets `ref->ct = NULL` and returns if empty, so using `CODETAG_EMPTY` avoids the warning.

The code mentions a size limit: `EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX` set to 256 page frame numbers (PFNs). If more than that many pages are allocated early, they get dropped and aren't cleared. It looks like this limit is in place for an early boot allocation list.

The commit message refers to setting the codetag to empty *after* `page_ext` initialization to avoid warnings when pages are later freed. The function that does this is `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`. It looks like this runs during initialization.

If `CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG` isn't enabled, `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()` and `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` effectively become no-ops, and `EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX` isn't defined. If profiling is *disabled*, `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` is a no-op, so those pages aren't added to the early list to be cleared. No warning since there is no tracking. I then considered the implications of the codetag being `NULL` in the absence of `DEBUG`. It appears that `alloc_tag_sub` will check for a `NULL` codetag and simply return, thus behaving as expected. The whole tracking system seems to be geared toward debugging with `DEBUG`.

I had to clarify whether `page_ext` is zero-initialized. `alloc_page_ext()` uses `kmemleak_alloc()` and handles initialization based on the `__GFP_ZERO` flag. A zero-initialized `ref->ct` would cause the `!ref->ct` warning in `alloc_tag_sub_check`.

Okay, so I'm looking at this code and immediately see a potential issue with the early memory profiling setup. Specifically, if `setup_early_mem_profiling` sets `mem_profiling_state` to `UP` too early, it's going to mess up the intended behavior during early boot. The problem is that this function, which seems to handle the initialization of memory profiling, is called during `early_param` which is way before `page_ext_init()` is called, and `page_ext_init()` is crucial for the memory tag infrastructure.

Let's trace this: `setup_early_mem_profiling` sets up some basic flags.  If it gets called (which it does via `early_param`), it flips `mem_profiling_support` to true and, critically, sets `mem_profiling_state` to `UP`. This happens through the command line option "sysctl.vm.mem_profiling".  And `early_param` is going to run in `parse_early_param()` inside `setup_arch()`, well before `page_ext_init()`, which is called later in `start_kernel()`.

The issue is that if `mem_profiling_state` is now `UP` because of the boot parameter, `mem_profiling_is_available()` will return true.  And that has huge implications for `__pgalloc_tag_add`.  That function conditionally calls `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` like so:

```c
		if (!mem_profiling_is_available())
			alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(page_to_pfn(page));
```

If `mem_profiling_is_available()` is true (which it is, because of the early parameter setting), that call is skipped! The implication is that we won't record the PFNs of those early allocations.

But wait, even if it *didn't* skip that, there's another check. If `page_ext` isn't available yet, `get_page_tag_ref` returns false. And in the `else` block, *that* does the check:

```c
	} else {
		/*
		 * page_ext is not available yet, record the pfn so we can
		 * clear the tag ref later when page_ext is initialized.
		 */
		if (!mem_profiling_is_available())
			alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(page_to_pfn(page));
	}
```

So even if the original check allowed it, that `else` block won't call `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn` because `mem_profiling_is_available()` is true if the early boot parameter is used!  If the boot parameter `sysctl.vm.mem_profiling` is given, this completely breaks the intention of the patch. The early PFNs won't be recorded!

So, what if the parameter *isn't* passed? In that case, `mem_profiling_state` stays at `DOWN` until `init_page_alloc_tagging` is called. But, as I've already pointed out, if the parameter *is* given, it becomes `UP` way too early. The early PFNs are not recorded. This deserves a closer look. If the tag is empty (because it wasn't set earlier), and `CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG` is enabled, a warning is triggered. The problem's root cause is that, during early allocation, the `codetag` isn't initialized.

My understanding is that `__pgalloc_tag_sub` calls `alloc_tag_sub`, which then checks for a valid tag. The patch introduces `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()` to record the PFNs (Physical Frame Numbers) of these early allocated pages. Then, `init_page_alloc_tagging()` calls `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` to clear the tag refs for these early allocations.

The essential process is to set an "empty" tag ref, which will avoid the warning. Examining `alloc_tag_add_early_pfn()` reveals a static array `early_pfns` of size 256. Let's trace `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`. It iterates through the recorded PFNs and calls `clear_page_tag_ref()` for each one. The page is freed early, and `__pgalloc_tag_sub()` safely does nothing since `page_ext` is not yet available.\n3. `page_ext_init()` runs, allocating and initializing the `page_ext` array for the memory zone containing this PFN.\n4. A concurrent or subsequent memory request (e.g., inside `alloc_page_ext()`) grabs memory from the buddy allocator and is handed this exact same PFN.\n5. Since `page_ext` is now available, `__pgalloc_tag_add()` successfully stores a valid allocation tag into the page's `page_ext` and increments the tag's `bytes` and `calls` profiling counters.\n6. `init_page_alloc_tagging()` then executes `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`, which loops over `early_pfns` and unconditionally calls `clear_page_tag_ref()` on the PFN.\n7. This permanently overwrites the newly written, valid allocation tag with `CODETAG_EMPTY`.\n8. When the new allocation is eventually freed, `alloc_tag_sub()` sees `CODETAG_EMPTY` and returns without decrementing the tag's counters, leading to an accounting leak. To fix this, the clearing logic must read the tag and verify `!ref.ct` before overwriting it."

      "reasoning": "1. If `CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG` is enabled, `CODETAG_EMPTY` is defined as `((void *)1)`.\n2. The patch uses `clear_page_tag_ref()` to set the tags of all `early_pfns` to `CODETAG_EMPTY`. For early allocations that are still active in the system, their valid `page_ext` entries now hold `((void *)1)`.\n3. If one of these active early allocations is a high-order page and is later split via `split_page()`, `pgalloc_tag_split()` will invoke `pgalloc_tag_get(&folio->page)`.\n4. `__pgalloc_tag_get()` reads the tag and checks `if (ref.ct)`. Since `((void *)1)` is truthy, it passes the check. It fails to call `is_codetag_empty(ref)` to catch the sentinel value.\n5. It passes `((void *)1)` to `ct_to_alloc_tag()`, which converts it to the invalid pointer `(struct alloc_tag *)1` (because `ct` is at offset 0 of `struct alloc_tag`).\n6. `pgalloc_tag_split()` checks `if (!tag)`, which passes, and calls `alloc_tag_ref_set()`, executing `this_cpu_inc(tag->counters->calls)`.\n7. Dereferencing `tag->counters` at `0x1 + offset` triggers an invalid page fault and kernel panic. `__pgalloc_tag_get()` must be updated to check `!is_codetag_empty(&ref)` before conversion, or active early allocations must not be forced to `CODETAG_EMPTY`."

    "thought": "**Analysis of Memory Allocation Tagging Vulnerabilities**

Alright, let's break this down. My initial assessment was nearly spot-on, but the devil, as always, is in the details. The core issue revolves around the handling of allocation tags, specifically when dealing with "early" allocations – those occurring before the `page_ext` structure is fully initialized – and what happens when those allocations are later manipulated, especially when CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG is defined.

The critical piece lies in `__pgalloc_tag_get()`. This function, intended to retrieve the allocation tag associated with a page, has a fundamental flaw. It *doesn't* adequately check if the tag is empty. When `CODETAG_EMPTY` is set, which happens during the `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` process when the `CONFIG_MEM_ALLOC_PROFILING_DEBUG` is defined, it's defined as `((void*)1)`. `__pgalloc_tag_get()` simply checks if `ref.ct` is truthy, which is the case for `((void*)1)`. Consequently, it *incorrectly* returns this garbage pointer as if it were a valid `struct alloc_tag*`.

This leads to two primary, very severe, concerns:

1.  **Memory Accounting Leaks**: The code is designed to track memory allocations. If `early_pfns` contains a PFN that gets freed and *reallocated* before the `page_ext` is initialized, the allocation tag is written. When `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` runs, it calls `clear_page_tag_ref()` setting `CODETAG_EMPTY` on it, overwriting the newly written tag. When the page is freed again, it will trigger the code `alloc_tag_sub` which fails to decrement the `bytes` and `calls` counters in the tag, because `CODETAG_EMPTY` is set! This creates a leak in the allocation accounting, which causes the memory profiler to provide inaccurate readings. This is because the code does not distinguish between a *freshly* allocated page and an *early* allocation being cleared. It fails to check if the tag exists before overwriting it.

2.  **Kernel Panic via Invalid Pointer Dereference**: If an early allocation remains active (i.e., not freed), and its tag is later cleared to `CODETAG_EMPTY` during `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()`, and that page is then subjected to a function like `pgalloc_tag_split()` or `pgalloc_tag_swap()` that calls `__pgalloc_tag_get()`, the incorrect pointer is passed to a function that uses it like `alloc_tag_ref_set` in `pgalloc_tag_split()` or `pgalloc_tag_sub_pages` in `pgalloc_tag_swap()`. This will attempt to dereference a memory address like `0x1` (plus some offset, depending on the structure layout). Since that memory isn't allocated, this triggers a kernel panic immediately.

The critical oversight is the missing check for `is_codetag_empty()` in `__pgalloc_tag_get()` and, perhaps, the unconditional setting of empty tags for early allocations that can be, and are, later reused. The original intent was likely sound—clear out old tags to avoid confusion—but the implementation introduces this critical vulnerability. And this affects BOTH high-order pages and regular pages! The memory profiler now introduces a severe system instability!

These are fundamental issues, and the fixes are relatively simple: ensure that `__pgalloc_tag_get()` checks for empty tags, and in `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` verify that `ref.ct` is empty before clearing it, to prevent invalid pointer dereferences.

    "content": "commit 91f34c42aef6e45e534fa70e0a72f2e6b5f26358
Author: Hao Ge <hao.ge@linux.dev>

mm/alloc_tag: clear codetag for pages allocated before page_ext initialization

This commit addresses an issue where pages allocated before page_ext is
fully initialized lack codetags, which triggers warnings when they are
later freed. It introduces a global array to track these early allocations
and clears their tags once page_ext initialization is complete.

> diff --git a/lib/alloc_tag.c b/lib/alloc_tag.c
> --- a/lib/alloc_tag.c
> +++ b/lib/alloc_tag.c
[ ... ]
> +void alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(unsigned long pfn)
> +{
> +	unsigned long flags;
> +
> +	if (mem_profiling_state != DOWN)
> +		return;
> +
> +	spin_lock_irqsave(&early_pfn_lock, flags);

Since this is called from the generic __pgalloc_tag_add() post-allocation
hook, can it be invoked from NMI context? If an NMI triggers an allocation
and interrupts a CPU already holding early_pfn_lock, will attempting to
acquire the same lock result in a deadlock?

> +	if (early_pfn_count >= EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX) {
> +		spin_unlock_irqrestore(&early_pfn_lock, flags);
> +		return;
> +	}

EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX is hardcoded to 256. If subsystems like SLUB fallback
trigger more than 256 allocations before page_ext is initialized, the array
will overflow and silently drop the remaining PFNs. Will these dropped
pages fail to have their tags cleared, eventually triggering the exact
warning this patch intends to fix?

[ ... ]
> +static void __init clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void)
> +{
> +	unsigned int i;
> +
> +	for (i = 0; i < early_pfn_count; i++) {
> +		unsigned long pfn = early_pfns[i];
